text_blocks.text: In the groups Z/nZ introduced above, the element 1 is
primitive, so these groups are cyclic. Indeed, each element is
expressible as a sum all of whose terms are 1. Any cyclic
group with n elements is isomorphic to this group. A second
example for cyclic groups is the group of n-th complex roots
of unity, given by complex numbers z satisfying zn - 1.
These numbers can be visualized as the vertices on a regular
n-gon, as shown in blue at the right for n - 6. The
group operation is multiplication of complex numbers. In the
picture, multiplying with a corresponds to a counter clockwise
rotation by 60deg. Using some field theory, the group Fox
can be shown to be cyclic: for example, if p = 5, 3 is a
